sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=28, col=46), RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 43, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Swap worked - left green now W center (inside left dark outline), right green c center (at white block). Level not complete. Right dark outline still visible.
- Plan: Try clicking on right dark outline again now that right green has c center - maybe the absorption condition depends on center colors.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 42 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After moving left green RIGHT:
- Left green moved from (33-35, 12-14) to (33-35, 15-17), center=W
- The left dark outline reappeared at (33-35, 12-14) - it's now visible again as a w (light gray) object
- Right green: (30-32, 30-32), center=c
- Right dark outline: (26-30, 44-48) - still visible
So the left green moved out of the left dark outline, and the outline reappeared. This confirms the absorption/release mechanic.
Now I see the left dark outline is at (33-35, 12-14) with w (light gray) color. Wait, that's different from before. Earlier the left dark outline was at (32-36, 11-15) with c (charcoal) color. Now it's at (33-35, 12-14) with w color.
Hmm, that's interesting. The outline changed position and color. Let me look more carefully.
Actually, looking at the segmentation:
- `color=w px=9 bound=[[33, 12], [33, 14], [35, 14], [35, 12]]` - this is a 3x3 object with w (light gray) color
- `color=N px=8 bound=[[33, 15], [33, 17], [35, 17], [35, 15]]` - this is the left green, 3x3
- `color=W px=1 bound=[[34, 16]]` - this is the center of the left green
Wait, the left green is at (33-35, 15-17) with N color and W center. The dark outline is at (33-35, 12-14) with w color.
But earlier, the left dark outline was at (32-36, 11-15) which is a 5x5 object. Now it's at (33-35, 12-14) which is a 3x3 object.
This is strange. The dark outline changed size and position. Let me think about what happened.
Actually, wait. When the left green was inside the dark outline, the outline was "absorbed" (not visible as a separate object). When the left green moved out, the outline reappeared. But the outline's position and size changed.
Hmm, maybe the outline "follows" the green? Or maybe the outline is a different object now?
Let me look at the image. The left panel shows:
- A dark outline (3x3) at the left
- A green square (3x3) to the right of the outline
The green is at (33-35, 15-17) and the outline is at (33-35, 12-14). They're adjacent horizontally.
Wait, but earlier the left dark outline was at (32-36, 11-15), which is a 5x5 object. Now it's at (33-35, 12-14), which is a 3x3 object. The outline shrank from 5x5 to 3x3.
Actually, I think I was misreading the segmentation earlier. Let me reconsider.
The left dark outline might have always been a 3x3 object, and the 5x5 boundary I saw earlier was including the green inside it.
OK, let me focus on what's important. The left green is now at (33-35, 15-17) with W center. The left dark outline is at (33-35, 12-14).
I need to figure out the goal. Let me try a completely different approach.
What if the goal is to have the green squares at specific positions with specific colors, and the dark outlines are just visual guides?
Looking at the current state:
- Left green: (33-35, 15-17), W center
- Right green: (30-32, 30-32), c center
- Left dark outline: (33-35, 12-14)
- Right dark outline: (26-30, 44-48)
What if the goal is to have:
- Left green at (33-35, 12-14) (inside left dark outline) with c center
- Right green at (27-29, 45-47) (inside right dark outline) with c center
But the right green can't reach the right dark outline. So this can't be the goal.
Let me try yet another hypothesis. What if the goal is to have both greens at the same row? Currently:
- Left green: rows 33-35
- Right green: rows 30-32
They're not at the same row. If I move the left green UP, it would be at rows 30-32, same as the right green.
Let me try: move left green UP (it has W center, so it can move).
Actually wait, the left green is at (33-35, 15-17). If I move UP, it goes to (30-32, 15-17). Then both greens would be at rows 30-32.
Let me try this.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Left green moved out of left dark outline (now at 33-35, 15-17 with W center). Left dark outline reappeared at (33-35, 12-14). Right green at white block (30-32, 30-32) with c center. Right dark outline still at (26-30, 44-48).
Plan: Try moving left green UP to align rows with right green (both at rows 30-32). Maybe the goal requires both greens at the same row.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Left green at (33-35, 15-17) with W center
# Move UP to align with right green at rows 30-32
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After UP:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 10:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
print("  level_completed:", result.get('level_completed'))

# If not complete, try RIGHT to move left green into left dark outline
if not result.get('level_completed'):
    result2 = action(['LEFT'])
    seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
    print("\nAfter LEFT:")
    for n in seg2['nodes']:
        if n['pixels'] <= 10:
            print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
    print("  level_completed:", result2.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
